articles.full_text:
CM Mohan Majhi Announces Early Summer Holidays for Schools Amid Heatwave
Amid rising heatwave conditions across Odisha, Chief Minister Mohan Charan Majhi has announced early summer vacations for schools from 27th April, 2026.
The decision comes as temperatures continue to soar across several parts of the state. The early closure has been declared as a precautionary measure to protect students from extreme weather conditions.
A day earlier, a high-level meeting chaired by the Revenue Minister, along with the Special Relief Commissioner (SRC), reviewed the situation and proposed a uniform holiday for schools across Odisha.
District collectors had also been instructed to visit affected areas, assess ground conditions, and evaluate the impact of rising temperatures on students before taking a final call. The move is aimed at ensuring the safety and well-being of children during the ongoing heatwave.
Author: MCL bureau
We are the core team of MyCityLinks. A team of dedicated persons to create some out of the box content

articles.title: Vegetable prices surge amid heat wave - The Hans India

articles.full_text:
Vegetable prices surge amid heat wave
Bengaluru
Rising temperatures across Karnataka have triggered a sharp increase in vegetable prices, leaving consumers struggling with higher household expenses. Over the past week, prices of several vegetables have risen by ₹10 to ₹40 per kilogram, driven largely by reduced supply and increased demand.
The ongoing heatwave has significantly impacted crop yield, leading to a drop in the quantity of vegetables arriving in markets. At the same time, demand from hotels and social events has gone up, further pushing prices higher. Beans, a staple ingredient in dishes like pulao and sambar, have seen one of the steepest hikes, now retailing at ₹100 per kg double last week’s price.
Vendors say the surge in prices is also affecting their business. Selvi, a vegetable seller, said that customers are cutting down on purchases due to high costs, making it difficult for traders to sustain sales. “As prices rise, people are buying less, and that directly affects our income,” she said.
Current market prices compared to last week show a steep upward trend. Tomatoes have increased from ₹20 to ₹35 per kg, carrots from ₹40 to ₹80, and cabbage from ₹30 to ₹60. Cauliflower prices have doubled from ₹10 to ₹20 per piece, while radish has gone up from ₹40 to ₹60. Potatoes now cost ₹50 per kg compared to ₹30 last week, and bitter gourd, ridge gourd, capsicum, and beetroot have all seen similar hikes.
Experts warn that if the heatwave continues, vegetable prices may rise even further in the coming days, adding to the burden on consumers already coping with extreme weather conditions.
